message.content: reacquaint

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

file.content:
# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

When a `## Todo` item is picked up, its text moves here: the
problem overview and its list of things to do. That is followed
by the "plan" — a bulleted list of the development "ladder":
   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation

**feat: passive bench, composable command grammar**

Today's `bench` mutates the device on every run
(configure → measure → restore), conflating measurement with
configuration, and reopens the port per subcommand — each reopen
a wedge die-roll. Redesign `bench` to be purely passive,
decompose config into composable `get-*`/`set-*` verbs, run a
whole CLI line over a single connection, and add file-backed
named states. Full design + rationale in chores-01 [[1]].

A code reorganization comes first: `main.rs` (~1890 lines) is
split into a lib crate of focused modules, so the redesign's
large new surface (verbs, step grammar, TOML config, named
states) lands in clean modules instead of growing the monolith.
Design + module layout in chores-01 [[5]].

   - 0.3.0-0 prep: land the design note + this entry. (done)
   - 0.3.0-1 refactor: add `lib.rs` + thin `main.rs`; move the
     VN-100 protocol primitives (checksum/CRC, command framing,
     register parsers, `Field`/`FIELDS`) to `proto.rs`. (done)
   - 0.3.0-2 refactor: move port I/O (`read_reply`, `transact`,
     `transact_retry`, `send_reboot_command`) to `transact.rs`.
     (done)
   - 0.3.0-3 refactor: move arg parsing, the `Command` enum, and
     help text to `cli.rs`; scaffold `bench.rs`. (done)
   - 0.3.0-4 passive bench: `bench [SECS]` measures the live
     stream only — drop the configure/measure/restore code;
     ASCII line-count + binary frame rate via a `0xFA` sniff
     (CRC-checked) + total wire throughput. Resolves the passive
     binary-rate open question [[1]] (0xFA sniff). Lands in
     `bench.rs`. (current)
   - 0.3.0-5 decompose config verbs: add `get-ascii`/`set-ascii`
     (reg 6) and `get-bin`/`set-bin` (reg 75) beside the
     existing `get-hz`/`set-hz`; bare-enable semantics.
   - 0.3.0-6 step grammar + one connection: shell-word steps,
     `+` token join, single port open, left-to-right execution,
     option-A resolve (merge `set-bin`+`set-hz` into one reg-75
     write).
   - 0.3.0-7 named states: `--config` TOML profile map;
     `save-state` / `set-state` / `restore-state`; default =
     bare-`restore-state` target, never auto-applied; baud
     excluded from restore.
   - 0.3.0 close-out: README + `--help` rewrite, validation
     (cargo cycle), version bump.

content.text:
Reacquainted. Here's where things stand:

## Position
- **Branch HEAD**, working copy clean and empty, parented on `main` → `7fd38e8 docs: fix ICD citations, add ARCHITECTURE.md`. Nothing in flight.
- `.claude` repo has the usual live session churn (expected).

## Active cycle — `feat: passive bench, composable command grammar` (0.3.0)
Redesign `bench` to be purely passive, decompose config into composable `get-*`/`set-*` verbs, run a whole CLI line over one connection, add file-backed named states. Design in chores-01 [[1]],[[5]].

**Ladder progress:**
- ✅ `0.3.0-0` prep — design note + entry
- ✅ `0.3.0-1` refactor: lib.rs + proto.rs (protocol primitives)
- ✅ `0.3.0-2` refactor: transact.rs (port I/O)
- ✅ `0.3.0-3` refactor: cli.rs + bench.rs scaffold
- ⏭️ **`0.3.0-4` passive bench (current)** — `bench [SECS]` measures the live stream only; drop configure/measure/restore; ASCII line-count + binary frame rate via `0xFA` sniff (CRC-checked) + total wire throughput. Lands in `bench.rs`.
- ⬜ `0.3.0-5` decompose config verbs (`get/set-ascii` reg 6, `get/set-bin` reg 75)
- ⬜ `0.3.0-6` step grammar + single connection
- ⬜ `0.3.0-7` named states (TOML profiles)
- ⬜ `0.3.0` close-out (README + `--help`, cargo cycle, version bump)

**Recently landed (Done):** RPi5 UART default + binary-port fix on TTL; bench silences async before binary config.

Next up is **0.3.0-4 passive bench**. Want me to start on it?

nps-gnc@rpi5-20cd 26-06-28T03:52:59.982Z:~/data/prgs/nps-gnc/rw-vn100 (main+1)

content.text:
That's the headline result — **200 Hz binary streaming, rock-solid at 921600.** Reading the run:

- **1000 frames in 5.00s = 199.9 Hz** against the 200 Hz target — that's the divisor-4 quantization (800 kHz IMU / 4 = 200, the 0.1 Hz shortfall is just rounding/measurement window), not drift. Nailed.
- **24% link utilization** (~220 kbit/s of 921.6). That's the "reliably" part — you're nowhere near saturating the wire, so there's comfortable headroom. The full 7-field frame is only 110 B.
- All seven fields decoding cleanly (time/ypr/quat/gyro/accel/imu/magpres), sample values look sane.

This is the original 200 Hz goal met with margin to spare, and at a baud that leaves 3/4 of the link unused — so even richer frames or a higher rate later isn't link-bound.

One note: that run is on `0.3.0-3.1` and still shows the old **configure → measure → restore** behavior (the `$VNWRG,75,...` writes before and after). That's exactly what **0.3.0-4 passive bench** is slated to strip out — going forward `bench` will just observe the live stream rather than mutating the device.

Want me to:
1. **Capture this 921600/200 Hz result** somewhere durable (a Done/chores note, or fold it into the 0.3.0-4 design rationale), and/or
2. **Start 0.3.0-4** now?

Or is this purely an FYI before we pick up the next step?

            ),

content.text:
Confirmed — it's in the `"imu"` arm of the per-field formatter (`src/bench.rs:64-72`):

```rust
"imu" => format!(
    "uncomp_accel=[{:.3}, {:.3}, {:.3}] uncomp_gyro=[{:.4}, {:.4}, {:.4}]",
    ...
),
```

**No, there's no deliberate reason — it's an oversight.** Every other arm tags its unit (`ypr ... deg`, `gyro ... rad/s`, `accel ... m/s^2`, `mag ... G`, `temp ... C`, `pres ... kPa`), and `quat` correctly has none (dimensionless). The `imu` arm is the only one carrying physical quantities without units.

The values *are* the same physical quantities as their compensated siblings, so the units should match:
- `uncomp_accel` → **m/s^2** (same as `accel`)
- `uncomp_gyro` → **rad/s** (same as `gyro`)

We think it slipped through because this one arm packs *two* vectors into a single format string, so it didn't visually line up with the one-quantity-per-arm pattern where the trailing unit is obvious.

The consistent version would be:

```rust
"uncomp_accel=[{:.3}, {:.3}, {:.3}] m/s^2 uncomp_gyro=[{:.4}, {:.4}, {:.4}] rad/s",
```

(matching the `.3`/`.4` precision already used for accel vs gyro, which is also consistent — good there.)

Want me to make that fix? It's a one-liner. Note it'd land naturally inside **0.3.0-4 passive bench**, which is reworking `bench.rs` anyway — though I can also do it as a standalone tidy if you'd rather not bundle it.

71	## feat: passive bench, composable command grammar
72	
73	Commits: [[6]]
74	
75	Today's `bench` is too complex: every run *mutates* the device
76	(configure → measure → restore), conflating measurement with
77	configuration, and each subcommand is its own process — one port
78	open per invocation, and each fresh-process reopen is a wedge
79	die-roll [[1]]. The redesign makes `bench` purely passive, breaks
80	configuration into composable `get-*`/`set-*` verbs, runs a whole
81	CLI line over a single connection, and adds file-backed named
82	states. We think running the line over one open cuts the
83	reconnect count that Issue #1 ties to wedges.
84	
85	- **Passive bench.** `bench [SECS]` measures whatever is already
86	  streaming — no device writes, no restore — default 5 s. This
87	  removes the configure→measure→restore dance that is bench's
88	  current bulk and its only wedge surface.
89	- **Decomposed config verbs**, each owning one register concern:
90	    - `get-hz` / `set-hz=<HZ>` — reg 7 async rate (exists today).
91	    - `get-ascii` / `set-ascii=<MODE|off>` — reg 6 ASCII preset.
92	    - `get-bin` / `set-bin=<FIELDS|off>` — reg 75 binary output.
93	    - Bare `set-bin` enables binary with the *current* fields;
94	      bare `set-ascii` enables ASCII with the current preset.
95	- **One connection per invocation** — the whole line opens the
96	  port once and runs its steps in order. See
97	  [Step grammar](#step-grammar-joined-tokens-space-separated-steps).
98	  Reading a register while that one connection is already streaming
99	  — and why rw-vn100 may discard stream data when it does — is in
100	  ARCHITECTURE.md [[9]].
101	- **Universal rate.** `set-hz=N` is a desired rate independent of
102	  output mode. See
103	  [Universal rate (option A)](#universal-rate-option-a).
104	- **File-backed named states.** A TOML config holds named
105	  profiles. See [Named states](#named-states-file-backed--default).
106	
107	### Step grammar: +-joined tokens, space-separated steps
108	
109	The CLI line is one open connection and a sequence of steps. The
110	grammar rides on shell word-splitting so there is no custom
111	space handling.
112	
113	- Each shell *word* is one **step**; steps execute left-to-right.
114	- Within a word, tokens joined by `+` apply together as one
115	  atomic unit; multiple `set-*` in the same word merge into a
116	  single device write.
117	- Comma stays reserved for value-internal lists
118	  (`set-bin=time,accel`); `+` is the only token joiner — never
119	  comma — so the grammar stays unambiguous and flat (the
120	  comma-nested alternative would make a value contain
121	  sub-assignments, i.e. recursion).
122	- Intra-step order is also left-to-right: `set-hz=50+bench` =
123	  apply, then measure.
124	- This unlocks a single-connection **sweep**:
125	  `save-state set-hz=50+bench set-hz=100+bench set-hz=200+bench
126	  restore-state` — snapshot, three configure-and-measure steps,
127	  restore, all on one open.
128	
129	### Universal rate (option A)
130	
131	`set-hz=N` reads as one desired rate regardless of mode; the
132	tool maps it to the right register.
133	
134	- ASCII: writes reg 7 directly.
135	- Binary: the rate is the rateDivisor *inside* reg 75 (`800/N`),
136	  the same register as the field mask — so `set-bin=…+set-hz=N`
137	  must resolve to one combined reg-75 write. The `+`-join is what
138	  makes that explicit: same word → one write.
139	- Bare `set-bin` with no `set-hz` in the same step keeps the
140	  device's current divisor.
141	
142	### Named states (file-backed) + default
143	
144	The `--config` file (default `./.rw-vn100-config.toml`, TOML,
145	hand-editable) holds a map of named profiles plus a `default`
146	key. Three verbs, three distinct jobs:
147	
148	- `save-state[=NAME]` — capture the current *device* regs into
149	  NAME (or the default name).
150	- `set-state=NAME+set-…` — define NAME from *explicit* values,
151	  no device read.
152	- `restore-state[=NAME]` — apply NAME (or the default when no
153	  name) *to* the device.
154	- `default` means only "the profile bare `restore-state` uses" —
155	  **never** auto-applied. The tool stays passive by default;
156	  nothing writes to the device unless a `set-*`/`restore-state`
157	  verb asks. We think an auto-applied default would reintroduce
158	  the surprise-mutation path Issue #1 [[1]] warns about.
159	- The snapshot covers output config (reg 6 / 7 / 75). Baud
160	  (reg 5) is excluded from restore by default, because rewriting
161	  baud is itself the wedge trigger; `--baud` controls the link
162	  instead.
163	
164	### Open questions (resolve before coding the relevant step)
165	
166	- `set-bin` and `set-ascii` both present on one line: error
167	  ("pick one output to bench") vs measure both — leaning error.
168	- Persist: apply volatile by default (reverts on power cycle,
169	  matches today's bench), with `--persist` available.
170	- `reset` / `factory-reset` reboot the device mid-line — constrain
171	  them to standalone / last.
172	- Passive binary frame-rate needs a frame length: reuse
173	  `get-bin`'s reg-75 parser to self-configure, or sniff the
174	  binary sync byte `0xFA`. v1 may report byte throughput +
175	  ASCII line rate and add decoded binary rate via the reg-75 read.
176	
177	
178	## refactor: split main.rs into lib modules
179	
180	Commits: [[7]],[[8]]
181	
182	`main.rs` had grown to ~1890 lines, and the 0.3.0 bench redesign
183	adds a large new surface (the `get-*`/`set-*` verbs, the `+`-step
184	grammar, a TOML config, named states). We split the monolith into
185	a lib crate of focused modules first, so the new code lands in
186	clean modules instead of growing the single file. Each step is a
187	pure code-move — behavior unchanged, verified by the existing
188	tests + clippy + a smoke run.
189	
190	- `proto.rs` — VN-100 protocol primitives: `checksum` /
191	  `vn_crc16`, command framing (`build_command`,
192	  `verify_checksum`), `error_description`, the register parsers,
193	  and the `Field`/`FIELDS` binary-output vocabulary.
194	- `transact.rs` — port I/O: `read_reply`, `transact`,
195	  `transact_retry`, `send_reboot_command`.
196	- `cli.rs` — `parse_args`, the `Command` enum, and help text.
197	- `bench.rs` — the `bench` command (passive, built in 0.3.0-4).
198	- `lib.rs` ties the modules together; `main.rs` is thin — parse
199	  args, open the port, dispatch.
200	- Ladder: proto (-1), transact (-2), cli + bench scaffold (-3);

## feat: passive bench, composable command grammar

Commits: [[6]]

Today's `bench` is too complex: every run *mutates* the device
(configure → measure → restore), conflating measurement with
configuration, and each subcommand is its own process — one port
open per invocation, and each fresh-process reopen is a wedge
die-roll [[1]]. The redesign makes `bench` purely passive, breaks
configuration into composable `get-*`/`set-*` verbs, runs a whole
CLI line over a single connection, and adds file-backed named
states. We think running the line over one open cuts the
reconnect count that Issue #1 ties to wedges.

- **Passive bench.** `bench [SECS]` measures whatever is already
  streaming — no device writes, no restore — default 5 s. This
  removes the configure→measure→restore dance that is bench's
  current bulk and its only wedge surface.
- **Decomposed config verbs**, each owning one register concern:
    - `get-hz` / `set-hz=<HZ>` — reg 7 async rate (exists today).
    - `get-ascii` / `set-ascii=<MODE|off>` — reg 6 ASCII preset.
    - `get-bin` / `set-bin=<FIELDS|off>` — reg 75 binary output.
    - Bare `set-bin` enables binary with the *current* fields;
      bare `set-ascii` enables ASCII with the current preset.
- **One connection per invocation** — the whole line opens the
  port once and runs its steps in order. See
  [Step grammar](#step-grammar-joined-tokens-space-separated-steps).
  Reading a register while that one connection is already streaming
  — and why rw-vn100 may discard stream data when it does — is in
  ARCHITECTURE.md [[9]].
- **Universal rate.** `set-hz=N` is a desired rate independent of
  output mode. See
  [Universal rate (option A)](#universal-rate-option-a).
- **File-backed named states.** A TOML config holds named
  profiles. See [Named states](#named-states-file-backed--default).

### Step grammar: +-joined tokens, space-separated steps

The CLI line is one open connection and a sequence of steps. The
grammar rides on shell word-splitting so there is no custom
space handling.

- Each shell *word* is one **step**; steps execute left-to-right.
- Within a word, tokens joined by `+` apply together as one
  atomic unit; multiple `set-*` in the same word merge into a
  single device write.
- Comma stays reserved for value-internal lists
  (`set-bin=time,accel`); `+` is the only token joiner — never
  comma — so the grammar stays unambiguous and flat (the
  comma-nested alternative would make a value contain
  sub-assignments, i.e. recursion).
- Intra-step order is also left-to-right: `set-hz=50+bench` =
  apply, then measure.
- This unlocks a single-connection **sweep**:
  `save-state set-hz=50+bench set-hz=100+bench set-hz=200+bench
  restore-state` — snapshot, three configure-and-measure steps,
  restore, all on one open.

### Universal rate (option A)

`set-hz=N` reads as one desired rate regardless of mode; the
tool maps it to the right register.

- ASCII: writes reg 7 directly.
- Binary: the rate is the rateDivisor *inside* reg 75 (`800/N`),
  the same register as the field mask — so `set-bin=…+set-hz=N`
  must resolve to one combined reg-75 write. The `+`-join is what
  makes that explicit: same word → one write.
- Bare `set-bin` with no `set-hz` in the same step keeps the
  device's current divisor.

### Named states (file-backed) + default

The `--config` file (default `./.rw-vn100-config.toml`, TOML,
hand-editable) holds a map of named profiles plus a `default`
key. Three verbs, three distinct jobs:

- `save-state[=NAME]` — capture the current *device* regs into
  NAME (or the default name).
- `set-state=NAME+set-…` — define NAME from *explicit* values,
  no device read.
- `restore-state[=NAME]` — apply NAME (or the default when no
  name) *to* the device.
- `default` means only "the profile bare `restore-state` uses" —
  **never** auto-applied. The tool stays passive by default;
  nothing writes to the device unless a `set-*`/`restore-state`
  verb asks. We think an auto-applied default would reintroduce
  the surprise-mutation path Issue #1 [[1]] warns about.
- The snapshot covers output config (reg 6 / 7 / 75). Baud
  (reg 5) is excluded from restore by default, because rewriting
  baud is itself the wedge trigger; `--baud` controls the link
  instead.

### Open questions (resolve before coding the relevant step)

- `set-bin` and `set-ascii` both present on one line: error
  ("pick one output to bench") vs measure both — leaning error.
- Persist: apply volatile by default (reverts on power cycle,
  matches today's bench), with `--persist` available.
- `reset` / `factory-reset` reboot the device mid-line — constrain
  them to standalone / last.
- Passive binary frame-rate needs a frame length: reuse
  `get-bin`'s reg-75 parser to self-configure, or sniff the
  binary sync byte `0xFA`. v1 may report byte throughput +
  ASCII line rate and add decoded binary rate via the reg-75 read.


## refactor: split main.rs into lib modules

Commits: [[7]],[[8]]

`main.rs` had grown to ~1890 lines, and the 0.3.0 bench redesign
adds a large new surface (the `get-*`/`set-*` verbs, the `+`-step
grammar, a TOML config, named states). We split the monolith into
a lib crate of focused modules first, so the new code lands in
clean modules instead of growing the single file. Each step is a
pure code-move — behavior unchanged, verified by the existing
tests + clippy + a smoke run.

- `proto.rs` — VN-100 protocol primitives: `checksum` /
  `vn_crc16`, command framing (`build_command`,
  `verify_checksum`), `error_description`, the register parsers,
  and the `Field`/`FIELDS` binary-output vocabulary.
- `transact.rs` — port I/O: `read_reply`, `transact`,
  `transact_retry`, `send_reboot_command`.
- `cli.rs` — `parse_args`, the `Command` enum, and help text.
- `bench.rs` — the `bench` command (passive, built in 0.3.0-4).
- `lib.rs` ties the modules together; `main.rs` is thin — parse
  args, open the port, dispatch.
- Ladder: proto (-1), transact (-2), cli + bench scaffold (-3);

content.content:
## Per-commit flow

Every commit (Preparation, each Work commit, Close-out) goes
through:

1. **Mark this commit `(current)`** as the first edit in
   `notes/todo.md > ## In Progress`.
2. **Do the work** (see [Iterative work](#iterative-work)
   for the loop-and-squash technique).
3. **Flip this commit `(current)` → `(done)`** in `## In
   Progress` — before the cargo cycle and the commit.
4. **Validate the artifact** — a medium-specific step, skip-able
   for notes-only commits, mandatory at close-out. For the Rust
   example the cargo cycle is:
   1. `cargo fmt`
   2. `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
   3. `cargo test`
   4. `cargo install --path . --locked`
   5. (re-test if anything substantive changed)
5. **Work review.** Stop *before* writing any description;
   tell the user "ready to commit." The user reviews the
   changes and we iterate until complete.
6. **Write the commit description** — see
   [Commit description](#commit-description).
7. **Commit Description review.** Show the title + body
   and stop. The user reviews the description. Iterate.
8. **Commit.** `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R .` for
   the app repo, `-R .claude` for the bot repo (`-R` last
   keeps the verb visible):

   ```
   jj commit -m \
   "<type>: <short description>" \
   -m "<intro paragraph>

   - file1: gist
   - file2: gist

   ochid: /.claude/<chid>" \
   -R .
   ```

**Two overrides apply:**

- **Deviation or question** — any time the work deviates
  from the agreed plan, or a question arises, stop and
  surface it; don't push through.
- **ESC-ESC** — the user can interrupt at any point to pull
  a review or question forward.

## Commit description

content.content:
## Step numbering

The cycle (Preparation -> Work -> Close-out; see
[cycle-protocol.md](cycle-protocol.md)) encodes each commit's
phase in the version suffix — the **final identifier `0` marks
a Preparation**:

- `X.Y.Z-0` — Preparation
- `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … — Work commits
- `X.Y.Z` — Close-out (bare version, no suffix)

**Preparation is optional.** A lightweight cycle — no ladder,
no setup commit — skips `-0` and starts at `-1` (its first
Work commit). The same holds at every level: a sub-cycle
needing no Preparation omits its `.0` (see Nesting). One that
grows a Preparation later adds the `0` step without
renumbering siblings.

Disambiguation:

- `-10` — Work commit #10 (final identifier `10`), not a
  Preparation.
- `-1.0` — Preparation of the `-1` sub-cycle (final
  identifier `0`).

**Nesting.** Sub-cycles append another level, recursively:

- `X.Y.Z-3.0` — Preparation of the `-3` sub-cycle
- `X.Y.Z-3.1`, `X.Y.Z-3.2` — its Work
- `X.Y.Z-3` — its Close-out
- `X.Y.Z-3.1.0` — Preparation of the `-3.1` sub-sub-cycle

Bump the version-of-record at the start of each phase, so the
active phase is recorded — and, per the preference above,
every commit carries a distinct version.

content.text: Now remove the orphaned private helpers in `cli.rs`. Let me read the top of the file and the helper definitions.

content.content:
1	//! Command-line surface — argument parsing, the `Command` enum, and help.
2	//!
3	//! - `parse_args` turns argv into a `Config` (port/baud) plus a `Command`.
4	//! - `help_text` renders usage; the ASCII-type and serial-port helpers map
5	//!   CLI names to register values.
6	
7	use crate::proto::*;
8	
9	/// Frequencies (Hz) the VN-100 accepts for the async data output rate.
10	/// Authoritative: REFERENCE.md "Register 7" (ICD §3.2.4 Table 3.9; vnsdk AsyncOutputFreq::Adof).
11	const VALID_RATES: &[u32] = &[1, 2, 4, 5, 10, 20, 25, 40, 50, 100, 200];
12	
13	/// Serial baud rates the VN-100 supports (register 5).
14	/// Authoritative: REFERENCE.md "Register 5" (ICD §3.2.2 Table 3.3; vnsdk BaudRate::BaudRates).
15	const VALID_BAUDS: &[u32] = &[
16	    9600, 19200, 38400, 57600, 115200, 128000, 230400, 460800, 921600,
17	];
18	
19	/// ASCII async message presets (register 6 ADOR): CLI name -> register value.
20	/// Authoritative: REFERENCE.md "Register 6" (ICD §3.2.3 Table 3.6;
21	/// vnsdk AsyncOutputType::Ador).
22	const ASCII_TYPES: &[(&str, u8)] = &[
23	    ("off", 0),
24	    ("ypr", 1),
25	    ("qtn", 2),
26	    ("qmr", 8),
27	    ("mag", 10),
28	    ("acc", 11),
29	    ("gyr", 12),
30	    ("mar", 13),
31	    ("ymr", 14), // the factory default
32	    ("yba", 16),
33	    ("yia", 17),
34	    ("imu", 19),
35	    ("dtv", 30),
36	    ("hve", 34),
37	];
38	
39	fn ascii_type_names() -> String {
40	    ASCII_TYPES
41	        .iter()
42	        .map(|(n, _)| *n)
43	        .collect::<Vec<_>>()
44	        .join(", ")
45	}
46	
47	/// Resolve a `--type` name (case-insensitive, optional `vn` prefix) to its ADOR.
48	fn parse_ascii_type(s: &str) -> Result<u8, String> {
49	    let lower = s.trim().to_lowercase();
50	    let key = lower.strip_prefix("vn").unwrap_or(&lower);
51	    ASCII_TYPES
52	        .iter()
53	        .find(|(n, _)| *n == key)
54	        .map(|(_, v)| *v)
55	        .ok_or_else(|| {
56	            format!(
57	                "unknown ASCII type `{s}`; choose from {}",
58	                ascii_type_names()
59	            )
60	        })
61	}
62	
63	/// Parse the `--serial-port` value into a register-75 `asyncMode` code.
64	///
65	/// - `1` / `2` target one of the VN-100's two UARTs; `both` (or `3`) targets
66	///   both. The default elsewhere is `2`, the RPi5 TTL header (the flight
67	///   target); the RS-232 bench is port 1. Selecting a port also subjects it to
68	///   the register-75 fit check, so `both` is avoided as a default — a port left
69	///   at a low baud would veto a frame the connected port could take.
70	fn parse_serial_port(s: &str) -> Result<u8, String> {
71	    match s.trim().to_lowercase().as_str() {
72	        "1" => Ok(1),
73	        "2" => Ok(2),
74	        "3" | "both" => Ok(3),
75	        other => Err(format!(
76	            "invalid --serial-port `{other}`; choose 1, 2, or both"
77	        )),
78	    }
79	}
80	
81	/// Display name for an ADOR value, e.g. 8 -> "VNYMR".
82	pub fn ascii_type_name(value: u8) -> String {
83	    ASCII_TYPES
84	        .iter()
85	        .find(|(_, v)| *v == value)
86	        .map(|(n, _)| {
87	            if *n == "off" {
88	                "off".to_string()
89	            } else {
90	                format!("VN{}", n.to_uppercase())
91	            }
92	        })
93	        .unwrap_or_else(|| format!("ADOR {value}"))
94	}

message.content: Hmm, how are we going to set the "--fields" for binary and "--type" for ascii and why two different parameters why not just use --fields but list/validate the two different valid sets for bin/ascii?

content.text:
Good — let me stop the deletion and answer this first, because it's really a **-5 design question** (the config verbs), and it's worth settling before I tear out the parsers that feed it.

## Why they're two different things today

The asymmetry isn't an arbitrary tool choice — it's forced by the VN‑100 ICD. The two outputs are controlled by two registers with fundamentally different *shapes*:

- **ASCII async = register 6 (ADOR), a pick‑*one* enum.** You choose a single named preset message. That's the `ASCII_TYPES` table I was about to delete — `ypr`, `qtn`, `ymr`, `imu`, … each is one `u8`, a whole canned message format. You cannot compose them; `VNYMR` is a *fixed bundle* (ypr+mag+accel+gyro). There is no "ASCII with just time+accel" mode in the device.
- **Binary = register 75, a pick‑*many* bitmask.** That's `FIELDS` — `time`, `ypr`, `quat`, `gyro`, `accel`, `imu`, `magpres`, each an independent bit. `--fields time,accel,gyro` is a composed subset (the mask we sniff in passive bench).

So one is **"select a preset" (enum)** and the other is **"compose a field set" (bitmask)**. Different verbs, different grammars, *disjoint vocabularies* (an ASCII preset name like `ymr` is not a binary field; a binary field like `magpres` is not an ASCII preset).

## Why a single `--fields` for both is worse, not better

A unified `--fields` that "validates against the right set per mode" would be **one flag hiding two grammars**:
- In binary mode it's a comma‑*list* of composable atoms.
- In ASCII mode it would have to be exactly *one* name from a different list — i.e. it's `--type` wearing `--fields`' clothes.

The flag's meaning would silently flip on the `--bin` mode switch, and the help could never show one coherent "Choices:" line. That's more surprising than two honestly‑named parameters.

## But — in the new design this mostly dissolves

In the passive‑bench redesign (chores‑01), neither is a bench flag anymore. They become **two verbs whose names encode the register**:
- `set-ascii=<preset|off>` → reg 6
- `set-bin=<fields|off>` → reg 75

The verb name *is* the disambiguator, so "why two parameters" answers itself — they're two different operations on two different registers. A single `set-output=…` would just reintroduce the same hidden mode‑switch problem.

---

So my recommendation is to keep the `set-ascii` / `set-bin` split as chores‑01 specifies. Given that, deleting `parse_ascii_type` / `parse_serial_port` in **-4** is still right — they'll be rewritten fresh as part of the `set-*` verbs in -5 regardless of which way we go.

Two things for you to decide before I continue:

1. **Keep the planned `set-ascii` / `set-bin` two-verb split?** (my rec: yes) — or do you want to explore a unified `set-output`/`--fields` after all?
2. For **-4 right now**: OK to delete those two parsers (they return in -5), or would you rather I leave them parked in place until -5 needs them?

content.text:
Yes — you've got it, and you've actually landed on exactly why passive bench is nicer than the old one.

**The two registers are independent and you configure them separately:**
- `set-ascii=<preset>` → reg 6 (ASCII async on/off + which preset)
- `set-bin=<fields>` → reg 75 (binary on/off + which fields)

(both arriving in -5). You set/initialize whichever you want — one, the other, or both — *then* bench.

**But bench itself needs no `--bin`/`--ascii` selector — that's the whole point of -4.** The new passive `bench` doesn't *configure* anything, so it has nothing to select. It just reads the wire and reports whatever is actually flowing, labeled:

```
ASCII:  200 messages in 5.00s = 40.0 Hz (...).
  Sample: $VNYMR,...
Binary: 1000 frames in 5.00s = 199.9 Hz (...).
  Fields: Common[time, ypr, ...] (110 B/frame).
  Sample: t=... ns, ...
Wire throughput ~220 kbit/s = 24% of the 921.6 kbit/s link.
```

If only binary is on, the ASCII line reads `ASCII:  none seen.`, and vice‑versa. So **"we'll see the appropriate items that run" happens automatically** — no flag required. Adding `--ascii`/`--bin` back to bench would re‑couple it to a mode it no longer needs to know about, which is exactly the coupling -4 removes.

A couple of nuances worth flagging:

- **Both can be live at once.** You can have ASCII async *and* binary streaming together, and bench will report both rates. (Caveat: they share the port's baud budget — the device's combined-load fit check means the *sum* has to fit the link. That's a `set-*` concern, not a bench concern.)
- Once the **step grammar** lands (-6), the configure‑then‑measure flow collapses to one line over one connection, e.g.:
  ```
  set-bin=time,accel,gyro+set-hz=200 bench
  ```
  but until then you'd just run `set-bin …` then `bench` as separate invocations.

So the design holds: two `set-*` verbs to configure, one mode‑agnostic passive `bench` to observe. Where I'd paused — deleting `parse_ascii_type` / `parse_serial_port` — is still the right move; they get rewritten as the `set-*` verbs in -5.

Shall I resume -4 with that deletion?

content.text:
Don't apologize — this is exactly the right time to raise it, before anything's committed. Having thought about it, **I think you're right, and I'd lean toward the swap.** Here's the honest reasoning.

## Why set-ascii / set-bin first is the better order

It's the **expand-then-contract** principle: build the replacement before removing the thing it replaces.

- **Today's working tool configures via `bench --bin --fields …`.** Passive bench (-4) *removes* that ability. But the replacement (`set-bin`/`set-ascii`) doesn't land until -5. So the current order has a one-step **capability gap**: between -4 and -5 the only way to turn binary on is raw `wrg 75 …`. Doing the verbs first means the tool is never less capable than it is right now.
- **Testability cuts the same way.** Passive bench is *hard to exercise* without a convenient way to turn binary on. Right after your 921600 run the device is back to ASCII-only @ 40 Hz (old bench restores), so a passive `bench` today would show binary "none seen." With `set-bin=time,accel,gyro` in hand first, validating passive bench is trivial: `set-bin …` then `bench`. Without it, I'm testing -4 through raw `wrg` or a hand-staged device state.

So: **verbs first gives you a continuously-capable tool *and* makes passive bench easy to validate when its turn comes.**

## The one wrinkle

The binary **rate** lives in reg 75 as a divisor (`800/N`), same register as the field mask. The clean "`set-bin=…+set-hz=N` → one combined write" depends on the `+`-join step grammar (-6). Before -6, `set-bin` can still land — it just writes the mask at the device's *current* divisor, and setting the binary rate standalone needs a read-modify-write of reg 75 (or you fall back to `wrg 75`). Workable, slightly less elegant. It doesn't block the swap; it just means -5's `set-hz`-for-binary story is a touch rougher until -6 tightens it.

## Proposed reordered ladder

- `0.3.0-4` **decompose config verbs** — `get/set-ascii` (reg 6), `get/set-bin` (reg 75). Keep the *old* bench as-is for now (parallel path).
- `0.3.0-5` **passive bench** — now safe to strip bench's configure/measure/restore, since the verbs replace it.
- `0.3.0-6` step grammar + one connection (incl. the merged reg-75 write).
- `0.3.0-7` named states.

## Cost of the swap right now

The in-progress -4 edits (Cargo.toml bump, the `bench.rs` passive rewrite, lib.rs dispatch, the partial cli.rs gutting) become premature. Nothing's committed, so reverting the working copy loses nothing permanent — and the passive-bench code is simple to reproduce when -5's turn comes. The tree is currently mid-surgery and doesn't compile anyway, so it needs a reset regardless.

**My recommendation: swap to verbs-first, revert the working copy to clean `main`, and start the new -4 (config verbs).** Want me to do that? If you'd rather keep passive-bench-first after all, say so and I'll just finish the -4 I started.

# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

When a `## Todo` item is picked up, its text moves here: the
problem overview and its list of things to do. That is followed
by the "plan" — a bulleted list of the development "ladder":
   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation

**feat: passive bench, composable command grammar**

Today's `bench` mutates the device on every run
(configure → measure → restore), conflating measurement with
configuration, and reopens the port per subcommand — each reopen
a wedge die-roll. Redesign `bench` to be purely passive,
decompose config into composable `get-*`/`set-*` verbs, run a
whole CLI line over a single connection, and add file-backed
named states. Full design + rationale in chores-01 [[1]].

A code reorganization comes first: `main.rs` (~1890 lines) is
split into a lib crate of focused modules, so the redesign's
large new surface (verbs, step grammar, TOML config, named
states) lands in clean modules instead of growing the monolith.
Design + module layout in chores-01 [[5]].

   - 0.3.0-0 prep: land the design note + this entry. (done)
   - 0.3.0-1 refactor: add `lib.rs` + thin `main.rs`; move the
     VN-100 protocol primitives (checksum/CRC, command framing,
     register parsers, `Field`/`FIELDS`) to `proto.rs`. (done)
   - 0.3.0-2 refactor: move port I/O (`read_reply`, `transact`,
     `transact_retry`, `send_reboot_command`) to `transact.rs`.
     (done)
   - 0.3.0-3 refactor: move arg parsing, the `Command` enum, and
     help text to `cli.rs`; scaffold `bench.rs`. (done)
   - 0.3.0-4 passive bench: `bench [SECS]` measures the live
     stream only — drop the configure/measure/restore code;
     ASCII line-count + binary frame rate via a `0xFA` sniff
     (CRC-checked) + total wire throughput. Resolves the passive
     binary-rate open question [[1]] (0xFA sniff). Lands in
     `bench.rs`. (current)
   - 0.3.0-5 decompose config verbs: add `get-ascii`/`set-ascii`
     (reg 6) and `get-bin`/`set-bin` (reg 75) beside the
     existing `get-hz`/`set-hz`; bare-enable semantics.
   - 0.3.0-6 step grammar + one connection: shell-word steps,
     `+` token join, single port open, left-to-right execution,
     option-A resolve (merge `set-bin`+`set-hz` into one reg-75
     write).
   - 0.3.0-7 named states: `--config` TOML profile map;
     `save-state` / `set-state` / `restore-state`; default =
     bare-`restore-state` target, never auto-applied; baud
     excluded from restore.
   - 0.3.0 close-out: README + `--help` rewrite, validation
     (cargo cycle), version bump.

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

- fix: bench silences async before binary config [[4]]

content.text:
Done — ladder swapped, `(current)` now on the new **-4 (config verbs)**, and the working copy is back to clean `main` (the old configure/measure `bench` is intact as the parallel path). The `-5` entry now notes it's exercisable via the `-4` verbs.

Before I write any code for the new -4, the config verbs have a few genuinely-open design points (chores-01 even flags some as "resolve before coding"). Here's my proposed shape — mostly settled by the design note, with two real forks for you.

## New -4: `get/set-ascii` (reg 6), `get/set-bin` (reg 75)

Settled by chores-01, my recommended semantics:
- `get-ascii` → read reg 6, print the preset (e.g. `VNYMR`).
- `set-ascii <preset|off>` → reg 6 ADOR; `off` writes 0.
- `get-bin` → read reg 75, print port / divisor / fields.
- `set-bin <fields|off>` → read-modify-write reg 75: `off` sets asyncMode 0; a field list sets the mask (groups=01) on port 2 (the RPi5 default), **preserving the current divisor**.
- **Persist**: volatile by default, `--persist` to write flash — same as today's `set-hz`/`baud`.

## Two forks I shouldn't guess on

**1. Argument syntax now, or the eventual `=`/`+` form?**
The chores design's final grammar is `set-bin=time,accel` (so `+`-joins compose in -6). But -4 lands before the step grammar.
- *(Rec)* **Positional now** — `set-bin time,accel`, matching today's `set-hz 40`. Migrate to `=`/`+` in -6. Keeps -4 small; the `=` form only earns its keep once `+`-joins exist.
- **Introduce `=` now** — `set-bin=time,accel` from the start, avoiding a later surface change.

**2. Is `set-hz` universal in -4, or still ASCII-only until -6?**
The binary rate is a divisor *inside* reg 75, so a universal `set-hz` for binary wants the merged reg-75 write that the `+`-join gives you in -6.
- *(Rec)* **ASCII-only in -4** (`set-hz` = reg 7, as today). `set-bin` keeps the current binary divisor; changing the binary *rate* waits for -6's `set-bin+set-hz` merge (or raw `wrg 75` meanwhile). This is the cleaner seam — but it means a fresh device can't hit 200 Hz *binary* through the verbs alone until -6.
- **Make `set-hz` universal now** — `set-hz 200` does a reg-75 read-modify-write when binary is active. More -4 code, pulls a chunk of -6 forward.

There's also one wrinkle to note (not a fork): reg 6 conflates ASCII on/off with the preset (0 = off), so there's no separate "enable" bit — I'd make **bare `set-ascii` an error** (nothing to enable without a preset), unlike bare `set-bin` which can re-enable a known mask. Shout if you'd rather it default to a preset.

How do you want forks 1 and 2?

content.text:
Good — and your #2 actually improves on the chores-01 plan. Let me lock in my read of #1, then lay out where #2 lands us.

**On #1** — I'm reading "argument syntax now" as **adopt the `=value` form now** (`set-bin=time,accel`), not positional. Correct me if you meant the positional style. It's the right call given #2: `set-bin-hz=200` reads naturally, and it's forward-compatible with the `+`-join grammar in -6 (`set-bin=time,accel+set-bin-hz=200` → one write). The older verbs (`rrg`, `wrg`, `baud`) stay positional for now; -6 unifies everything under the token grammar.

**On #2** — yes, I like explicit per-mode rate verbs, and they **supersede chores-01's "Universal rate (option A)."** Option A made `set-hz` mode-dependent and needed the `+`-join to merge a binary rate into the reg-75 write. Your split sidesteps all of that: each verb owns exactly one register concern, and — the nice part — **it kills fork 2's downside.** `set-bin-hz` can do its own reg-75 read-modify-write standalone, so a fresh device reaches 200 Hz *binary* through the verbs in -4, no -6 dependency.

Resulting verb set for -4 (all `key=value`):

| verb | register | meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `get-ascii` | reg 6 | read the ASCII preset |
| `set-ascii=<preset\|off>` | reg 6 | set ASCII preset / disable |
| `set-ascii-hz=<N>` | reg 7 | ASCII async rate |
| `get-bin` | reg 75 | read port / rate / fields |
| `set-bin=<fields\|off>` | reg 75 mask | set binary fields / disable |
| `set-bin-hz=<N>` | reg 75 divisor | binary rate (writes `800/N`) |

Two consequences to confirm:

1. **`set-hz` / `get-hz` are now redundant** — they're just `set-ascii-hz` / reg 7 under the old name. My rec: **rename to `set-ascii-hz` / `get-ascii-hz`**, and keep bare `set-hz`/`get-hz` as back-compat aliases for this cycle (fc.py or scripts may use them) — or drop them outright if you'd rather not carry aliases. Which?

2. **I'll update chores-01**: replace the "Universal rate (option A)" subsection with this per-mode-verb design (and note option A was considered and dropped), so the design note matches what we build. That edit lands as part of the -4 prep.

Once you pick the `set-hz` fate (alias vs drop) and confirm the `=` reading, I'll write it up as the -4 plan and start.

content.text:
"Drop" it is — no aliases, `get-hz`/`set-hz` go away entirely, replaced by `get-ascii-hz` / `set-ascii-hz`. And I'll take the `=` reading as confirmed.

That settles the design. -4 is sizable (6 verbs, a new `=`-token command parser, a reg-75 reader, plus the design-note revision), so I'll run it as a small sub-cycle. First the prep: update the design note so it matches what we're building, before any code. Let me re-read the exact chores-01 passages I'm revising.

content.content:
85	- **Passive bench.** `bench [SECS]` measures whatever is already
86	  streaming — no device writes, no restore — default 5 s. This
87	  removes the configure→measure→restore dance that is bench's
88	  current bulk and its only wedge surface.
89	- **Decomposed config verbs**, each owning one register concern:
90	    - `get-hz` / `set-hz=<HZ>` — reg 7 async rate (exists today).
91	    - `get-ascii` / `set-ascii=<MODE|off>` — reg 6 ASCII preset.
92	    - `get-bin` / `set-bin=<FIELDS|off>` — reg 75 binary output.
93	    - Bare `set-bin` enables binary with the *current* fields;
94	      bare `set-ascii` enables ASCII with the current preset.
95	- **One connection per invocation** — the whole line opens the
96	  port once and runs its steps in order. See
97	  [Step grammar](#step-grammar-joined-tokens-space-separated-steps).
98	  Reading a register while that one connection is already streaming
99	  — and why rw-vn100 may discard stream data when it does — is in
100	  ARCHITECTURE.md [[9]].
101	- **Universal rate.** `set-hz=N` is a desired rate independent of
102	  output mode. See
103	  [Universal rate (option A)](#universal-rate-option-a).
104	- **File-backed named states.** A TOML config holds named
105	  profiles. See [Named states](#named-states-file-backed--default).
106	
107	### Step grammar: +-joined tokens, space-separated steps
108	
109	The CLI line is one open connection and a sequence of steps. The
110	grammar rides on shell word-splitting so there is no custom
111	space handling.
112	
113	- Each shell *word* is one **step**; steps execute left-to-right.
114	- Within a word, tokens joined by `+` apply together as one
115	  atomic unit; multiple `set-*` in the same word merge into a
116	  single device write.
117	- Comma stays reserved for value-internal lists
118	  (`set-bin=time,accel`); `+` is the only token joiner — never
119	  comma — so the grammar stays unambiguous and flat (the
120	  comma-nested alternative would make a value contain
121	  sub-assignments, i.e. recursion).
122	- Intra-step order is also left-to-right: `set-hz=50+bench` =
123	  apply, then measure.
124	- This unlocks a single-connection **sweep**:
125	  `save-state set-hz=50+bench set-hz=100+bench set-hz=200+bench
126	  restore-state` — snapshot, three configure-and-measure steps,
127	  restore, all on one open.
128	
129	### Universal rate (option A)
130	
131	`set-hz=N` reads as one desired rate regardless of mode; the
132	tool maps it to the right register.
133	
134	- ASCII: writes reg 7 directly.
135	- Binary: the rate is the rateDivisor *inside* reg 75 (`800/N`),
136	  the same register as the field mask — so `set-bin=…+set-hz=N`
137	  must resolve to one combined reg-75 write. The `+`-join is what
138	  makes that explicit: same word → one write.
139	- Bare `set-bin` with no `set-hz` in the same step keeps the
140	  device's current divisor.
141	
142	### Named states (file-backed) + default
143	
144	The `--config` file (default `./.rw-vn100-config.toml`, TOML,
145	hand-editable) holds a map of named profiles plus a `default`
146	key. Three verbs, three distinct jobs:
147	
148	- `save-state[=NAME]` — capture the current *device* regs into
149	  NAME (or the default name).
150	- `set-state=NAME+set-…` — define NAME from *explicit* values,
151	  no device read.
152	- `restore-state[=NAME]` — apply NAME (or the default when no
153	  name) *to* the device.
154	- `default` means only "the profile bare `restore-state` uses" —
155	  **never** auto-applied. The tool stays passive by default;
156	  nothing writes to the device unless a `set-*`/`restore-state`
157	  verb asks. We think an auto-applied default would reintroduce
158	  the surprise-mutation path Issue #1 [[1]] warns about.
159	- The snapshot covers output config (reg 6 / 7 / 75). Baud
160	  (reg 5) is excluded from restore by default, because rewriting
161	  baud is itself the wedge trigger; `--baud` controls the link
162	  instead.
163	
164	### Open questions (resolve before coding the relevant step)

file.content:
- **Passive bench.** `bench [SECS]` measures whatever is already
  streaming — no device writes, no restore — default 5 s. This
  removes the configure→measure→restore dance that is bench's
  current bulk and its only wedge surface.
- **Decomposed config verbs**, each owning one register concern:
    - `get-hz` / `set-hz=<HZ>` — reg 7 async rate (exists today).
    - `get-ascii` / `set-ascii=<MODE|off>` — reg 6 ASCII preset.
    - `get-bin` / `set-bin=<FIELDS|off>` — reg 75 binary output.
    - Bare `set-bin` enables binary with the *current* fields;
      bare `set-ascii` enables ASCII with the current preset.
- **One connection per invocation** — the whole line opens the
  port once and runs its steps in order. See
  [Step grammar](#step-grammar-joined-tokens-space-separated-steps).
  Reading a register while that one connection is already streaming
  — and why rw-vn100 may discard stream data when it does — is in
  ARCHITECTURE.md [[9]].
- **Universal rate.** `set-hz=N` is a desired rate independent of
  output mode. See
  [Universal rate (option A)](#universal-rate-option-a).
- **File-backed named states.** A TOML config holds named
  profiles. See [Named states](#named-states-file-backed--default).

### Step grammar: +-joined tokens, space-separated steps

The CLI line is one open connection and a sequence of steps. The
grammar rides on shell word-splitting so there is no custom
space handling.

- Each shell *word* is one **step**; steps execute left-to-right.
- Within a word, tokens joined by `+` apply together as one
  atomic unit; multiple `set-*` in the same word merge into a
  single device write.
- Comma stays reserved for value-internal lists
  (`set-bin=time,accel`); `+` is the only token joiner — never
  comma — so the grammar stays unambiguous and flat (the
  comma-nested alternative would make a value contain
  sub-assignments, i.e. recursion).
- Intra-step order is also left-to-right: `set-hz=50+bench` =
  apply, then measure.
- This unlocks a single-connection **sweep**:
  `save-state set-hz=50+bench set-hz=100+bench set-hz=200+bench
  restore-state` — snapshot, three configure-and-measure steps,
  restore, all on one open.

### Universal rate (option A)

`set-hz=N` reads as one desired rate regardless of mode; the
tool maps it to the right register.

- ASCII: writes reg 7 directly.
- Binary: the rate is the rateDivisor *inside* reg 75 (`800/N`),
  the same register as the field mask — so `set-bin=…+set-hz=N`
  must resolve to one combined reg-75 write. The `+`-join is what
  makes that explicit: same word → one write.
- Bare `set-bin` with no `set-hz` in the same step keeps the
  device's current divisor.

### Named states (file-backed) + default

The `--config` file (default `./.rw-vn100-config.toml`, TOML,
hand-editable) holds a map of named profiles plus a `default`
key. Three verbs, three distinct jobs:

- `save-state[=NAME]` — capture the current *device* regs into
  NAME (or the default name).
- `set-state=NAME+set-…` — define NAME from *explicit* values,
  no device read.
- `restore-state[=NAME]` — apply NAME (or the default when no
  name) *to* the device.
- `default` means only "the profile bare `restore-state` uses" —
  **never** auto-applied. The tool stays passive by default;
  nothing writes to the device unless a `set-*`/`restore-state`
  verb asks. We think an auto-applied default would reintroduce
  the surprise-mutation path Issue #1 [[1]] warns about.
- The snapshot covers output config (reg 6 / 7 / 75). Baud
  (reg 5) is excluded from restore by default, because rewriting
  baud is itself the wedge trigger; `--baud` controls the link
  instead.

### Open questions (resolve before coding the relevant step)

file.content:
### VN-100 register-75 serial-port numbering on TTL

The VN-100 binary-output register (75) names a target serial port
in its first field (`asyncMode`): 1, 2, or 3 (both). ASCII async
(registers 6/7) instead targets whichever port the config command
arrived on, so it needs no port number and "just works" on the
connected wire — which is why `get-hz` and the ASCII `bench`
succeeded immediately. `rw-vn100` hardcodes `asyncMode=1`
(`main.rs:1060`, `bench_binary`), which on the Pi's wire emits no
binary at all.

Measured on `/dev/ttyAMA0`, identical field set (time+accel,
200 Hz), 2 s raw capture via `../fc/scripts/serial_check.py read`:

- `asyncMode=1` → 9760 B, ASCII `$VNYMR` only, no `0xFA` frames.
- `asyncMode=2` → 20145 B, `0xFA` binary frames present.
- `asyncMode=3` → 20151 B, `0xFA` binary frames present.

So the Pi's serial port (its one TX/RX pair on GPIO14/15) is the
VN-100's serial port **2** in the register-75 numbering; the bench
RS-232 rig used port 1.

Port 1 and port 2 are two *separate physical UART interfaces* on
the VN-100 — different pin sets — not two logical streams sharing
one line. We are wired to exactly one of them (port 2); port 1 is
the interface the bench RS-232 transceiver used, and the Pi is not
connected to it at all. What *is* interleaved on our one port is
two message *types*, not two ports:

input.command: sed -n '/## fix: bench silences async before binary config/,/^## /p' notes/chores/chores-01.md | head -60

content.content:
## fix: bench silences async before binary config

Commits: [[5]]

`bench --bin` could reject a binary config with `$VNERR` 0x0C
("insufficient baud rate") at a low baud even when the binary stream
fits the link with room to spare. The cause was write ordering:
bench configured the binary output (reg 75) while ASCII async (reg
7) was still streaming, so the device's fit check saw the *combined*
ASCII + binary load. The fix silences ASCII async before writing the
binary config, so the check sees only the binary load.

### Bench combined-load fit check at low baud

Observed 2026-06-26 on the RPi5 TTL header (`/dev/ttyAMA0`) running
`bench --bin` after an in-session baud switch to 57600:

- At 57600 with ASCII async still at 40 Hz, `VNWRG,75,2,20,01,0101`
  (time+accel, 26 B/frame, 40 Hz) was rejected with `$VNERR` 0x0C.
- The binary stream alone is ~10 kbit/s — 18% of the 57600 link —
  so it fits easily; only the transient ASCII-40 + binary-40
  combination overran the check.
- Lowering ASCII async to 4 Hz first, then re-running the identical
  bench, the same `VNWRG,75` was accepted and streamed clean at
  39.9 Hz. Only the ASCII rate changed, isolating the combined load
  as the cause.

We think the device enforces `message_size × rate ≤ baud` on every
reg-5 / reg-7 / reg-75 write, against the *sum* of the streams
configured on that port. At 115200 the headroom hid the transient;
at 57600 it surfaced. The ordering was always latent and only became
visible at the lower baud.

The fix reorders `bench_binary`:

- Before: read reg 7 → write reg 75 → write reg 7=0. The fit check
  fired on the reg-75 write, before ASCII was silenced.
- After: read reg 7 → write reg 7=0 → write reg 75, and restore reg
  7 if the reg-75 write still fails — so a genuinely-too-big binary
  frame (e.g. the 38 B frame at 200 Hz / 115200, [[2]]) no longer
  leaves ASCII async switched off.
- `bench_ascii` is unaffected: it has a single stream and already
  writes the message type (reg 6) before the rate (reg 7).

## Rename rdwr_vn100 to rw-vn100

lastPrompt: Did I understand that on the same serial port we can have bin and ascii streams at the same time?

content.text:
Yes — and it's confirmed by measurement, not just the spec. Two independent pieces of evidence in chores-01:

**1. They interleave on one physical port.** ASCII async (regs 6/7) and binary (reg 75) are *message types*, not separate ports. From the port-numbering investigation:

> What *is* interleaved on our one port is two message *types*, not two ports

ASCII async targets whichever port the command arrived on; binary's `asyncMode` names a port. Point both at the same UART (our port 2 on `/dev/ttyAMA0`) and `$VN…` lines and `0xFA` frames come out the same wire, interleaved.

**2. The device itself treats them as a combined load on that port.** This is the strongest proof — measured 2026-06-26 at 57600 baud:

- ASCII async at 40 Hz **plus** a binary 40 Hz config → device rejected the binary write with `$VNERR 0x0C` because the fit check saw the **sum** of both streams.
- Drop ASCII to 4 Hz, re-run the identical binary write → accepted, streamed clean at 39.9 Hz.

If they couldn't coexist on one port there'd be no "sum" to overflow. We think the device enforces `message_size × rate ≤ baud` against the *total* of every stream configured on that port.

So: **yes, both at once on the same port, sharing the baud budget.** This is actually why the passive `bench` I'd drafted scans for ASCII lines *and* binary frames over the same bytes and reports both — that design is the right shape precisely because both can be live.

One design consequence worth flagging for -4, though — it's the flip side of your question: when `set-bin` writes reg 75 while ASCII async is *still streaming*, that same combined fit-check can reject it at low baud even though binary alone fits. The old `bench` dodged this by silencing ASCII first. But a standalone `set-bin` verb silently turning ASCII off would be a surprise mutation (exactly the passivity we're protecting). So we'll need to decide how `set-bin` handles a `$VNERR 0x0C`: surface it with a hint ("ASCII async is consuming the budget — lower it or turn it off first") rather than auto-silencing. Want me to fold that into the -4 design now, or park it as an open question until we hit it?

file.content:
(`set-bin=time,accel`); `+` is the only token joiner — never
  comma — so the grammar stays unambiguous and flat (the
  comma-nested alternative would make a value contain
  sub-assignments, i.e. recursion).
- Intra-step order is also left-to-right: `set-hz=50+bench` =
  apply, then measure.
- This unlocks a single-connection **sweep**:
  `save-state set-hz=50+bench set-hz=100+bench set-hz=200+bench
  restore-state` — snapshot, three configure-and-measure steps,
  restore, all on one open.

### Universal rate (option A)

`set-hz=N` reads as one desired rate regardless of mode; the
tool maps it to the right register.

- ASCII: writes reg 7 directly.
- Binary: the rate is the rateDivisor *inside* reg 75 (`800/N`),
  the same register as the field mask — so `set-bin=…+set-hz=N`
  must resolve to one combined reg-75 write. The `+`-join is what
  makes that explicit: same word → one write.
- Bare `set-bin` with no `set-hz` in the same step keeps the
  device's current divisor.

### Named states (file-backed) + default

The `--config` file (default `./.rw-vn100-config.toml`, TOML,
hand-editable) holds a map of named profiles plus a `default`
key. Three verbs, three distinct jobs:

- `save-state[=NAME]` — capture the current *device* regs into
  NAME (or the default name).
- `set-state=NAME+set-…` — define NAME from *explicit* values,
  no device read.
- `restore-state[=NAME]` — apply NAME (or the default when no
  name) *to* the device.
- `default` means only "the profile bare `restore-state` uses" —
  **never** auto-applied. The tool stays passive by default;
  nothing writes to the device unless a `set-*`/`restore-state`
  verb asks. We think an auto-applied default would reintroduce
  the surprise-mutation path Issue #1 [[1]] warns about.
- The snapshot covers output config (reg 6 / 7 / 75). Baud
  (reg 5) is excluded from restore by default, because rewriting
  baud is itself the wedge trigger; `--baud` controls the link
  instead.

### Open questions (resolve before coding the relevant step)

- `set-bin` and `set-ascii` both present on one line: error
  ("pick one output to bench") vs measure both — leaning error.
- Persist: apply volatile by default (reverts on power cycle,
  matches today's bench), with `--persist` available.
- `reset` / `factory-reset` reboot the device mid-line — constrain
  them to standalone / last.
- Passive binary frame-rate needs a frame length: reuse
  `get-bin`'s reg-75 parser to self-configure, or sniff the
  binary sync byte `0xFA`. v1 may report byte throughput +
  ASCII line rate and add decoded binary rate via the reg-75 read.

  `save-state set-hz=50+bench set-hz=100+bench set-hz=200+bench

  restore, all on one open.

### Universal rate (option A)

`set-hz=N` reads as one desired rate regardless of mode; the
tool maps it to the right register.

- ASCII: writes reg 7 directly.
- Binary: the rate is the rateDivisor *inside* reg 75 (`800/N`),
  the same register as the field mask — so `set-bin=…+set-hz=N`
  must resolve to one combined reg-75 write. The `+`-join is what

input.new_string:
- Intra-step order is also left-to-right: `set-ascii-hz=50+bench`
  = apply, then measure.
- This unlocks a single-connection **sweep**:
  `save-state set-bin-hz=50+bench set-bin-hz=100+bench
  set-bin-hz=200+bench restore-state` — snapshot, three
  configure-and-measure steps, restore, all on one open.

### Per-mode rate verbs

Each output mode carries its rate in a different register, so the
rate is an explicit per-mode verb rather than one mode-dependent
`set-hz`.

- `set-ascii-hz=N` writes reg 7 (the ASCII async rate).
- `set-bin-hz=N` writes the rateDivisor (`800/N`) *inside* reg 75
  — a read-modify-write that preserves the field mask. Because it
  stands alone, the binary rate is settable without the `+`-join,
  so a device reaches e.g. 200 Hz binary through the verbs in the
  config-verbs step, not only once the step grammar lands.
- `set-bin` and `set-bin-hz` both touch reg 75. Run separately
  they are two writes (mask, then divisor); the `+`-join can merge
  `set-bin=…+set-bin-hz=N` into one write as an optimization, but
  correctness never depends on it.
- We considered a single **universal `set-hz`** (option A) mapping
  to reg 7 or the reg-75 divisor by mode, and dropped it: it made
  one verb mode-dependent and *required* the `+`-join to fold a
  binary rate into the reg-75 write, coupling the rate story to
  the step grammar. Per-mode verbs are each one register concern
  and stand alone.

content.text:
Parked — it's now an open question in chores-01, with the lean recorded (surface the `0x0C` with a hint, don't auto-silence ASCII). The design note is internally consistent again (subsection renamed, anchor fixed, `set-hz` examples updated).

On your question — **yes, your understanding is correct: you pick one stream, you don't need both.** But let me sharpen the "either can deliver" part, because it's not quite symmetric:

- **Binary (reg 75) — the flexible one.** You compose an *arbitrary* field set via the bitmask (`time`, `ypr`, `quat`, `gyro`, `accel`, `imu`, `magpres`, …), at full `f32` precision, in a compact frame. It can deliver essentially *any* data set you ask for. This is why fc.py runs binary at 200 Hz — it's the only one that's both complete and efficient enough.
- **ASCII (reg 6) — fixed bundles.** You pick *one preset* from a menu (`VNYMR`, `VNQMR`, `VNIMU`, …). Each is a canned bundle — you can't compose. The numbers are rounded text, and it's ~2× the bytes on the wire.

So the asymmetry: **binary can always deliver "all the data" you want; ASCII can deliver it only if a preset happens to match your desired fields.** "Either can deliver that" holds for the common cases a preset covers (ypr/mag/accel/gyro), but binary is strictly more capable.

Running **both at once is purely redundant for data delivery** — same information twice, doubling the wire load against that shared baud budget. The *only* reason you'd ever do it is a niche convenience: a low-rate human-readable ASCII feed for eyeballing, alongside the compact binary feed your code consumes. That's a monitoring choice, never a data-completeness need.

So for the flight controller: one stream, binary. The two `set-*` verbs being independent just means you *can* have both live — not that you'd want to.

Where to next — ready to move from design into coding the -4 verbs, or more to talk through first?

1	//! Command-line surface — argument parsing, the `Command` enum, and help.
2	//!
3	//! - `parse_args` turns argv into a `Config` (port/baud) plus a `Command`.
4	//! - `help_text` renders usage; the ASCII-type and serial-port helpers map
5	//!   CLI names to register values.
6	
7	use crate::proto::*;
8	
9	/// Frequencies (Hz) the VN-100 accepts for the async data output rate.
10	/// Authoritative: REFERENCE.md "Register 7" (ICD §3.2.4 Table 3.9; vnsdk AsyncOutputFreq::Adof).
11	const VALID_RATES: &[u32] = &[1, 2, 4, 5, 10, 20, 25, 40, 50, 100, 200];
12	
13	/// Serial baud rates the VN-100 supports (register 5).
14	/// Authoritative: REFERENCE.md "Register 5" (ICD §3.2.2 Table 3.3; vnsdk BaudRate::BaudRates).
15	const VALID_BAUDS: &[u32] = &[
16	    9600, 19200, 38400, 57600, 115200, 128000, 230400, 460800, 921600,
17	];
18	
19	/// ASCII async message presets (register 6 ADOR): CLI name -> register value.
20	/// Authoritative: REFERENCE.md "Register 6" (ICD §3.2.3 Table 3.6;
21	/// vnsdk AsyncOutputType::Ador).
22	const ASCII_TYPES: &[(&str, u8)] = &[
23	    ("off", 0),
24	    ("ypr", 1),
25	    ("qtn", 2),
26	    ("qmr", 8),
27	    ("mag", 10),
28	    ("acc", 11),
29	    ("gyr", 12),
30	    ("mar", 13),
31	    ("ymr", 14), // the factory default
32	    ("yba", 16),
33	    ("yia", 17),
34	    ("imu", 19),
35	    ("dtv", 30),
36	    ("hve", 34),
37	];
38	
39	fn ascii_type_names() -> String {
40	    ASCII_TYPES
41	        .iter()
42	        .map(|(n, _)| *n)
43	        .collect::<Vec<_>>()
44	        .join(", ")
45	}
46	
47	/// Resolve a `--type` name (case-insensitive, optional `vn` prefix) to its ADOR.
48	fn parse_ascii_type(s: &str) -> Result<u8, String> {
49	    let lower = s.trim().to_lowercase();
50	    let key = lower.strip_prefix("vn").unwrap_or(&lower);
51	    ASCII_TYPES
52	        .iter()
53	        .find(|(n, _)| *n == key)
54	        .map(|(_, v)| *v)
55	        .ok_or_else(|| {
56	            format!(
57	                "unknown ASCII type `{s}`; choose from {}",
58	                ascii_type_names()
59	            )
60	        })
61	}
62	
63	/// Parse the `--serial-port` value into a register-75 `asyncMode` code.
64	///
65	/// - `1` / `2` target one of the VN-100's two UARTs; `both` (or `3`) targets
66	///   both. The default elsewhere is `2`, the RPi5 TTL header (the flight
67	///   target); the RS-232 bench is port 1. Selecting a port also subjects it to
68	///   the register-75 fit check, so `both` is avoided as a default — a port left
69	///   at a low baud would veto a frame the connected port could take.
70	fn parse_serial_port(s: &str) -> Result<u8, String> {
71	    match s.trim().to_lowercase().as_str() {
72	        "1" => Ok(1),
73	        "2" => Ok(2),
74	        "3" | "both" => Ok(3),
75	        other => Err(format!(
76	            "invalid --serial-port `{other}`; choose 1, 2, or both"
77	        )),
78	    }
79	}
80	
81	/// Display name for an ADOR value, e.g. 8 -> "VNYMR".
82	pub fn ascii_type_name(value: u8) -> String {
83	    ASCII_TYPES
84	        .iter()
85	        .find(|(_, v)| *v == value)
86	        .map(|(n, _)| {
87	            if *n == "off" {
88	                "off".to_string()
89	            } else {
90	                format!("VN{}", n.to_uppercase())
91	            }
92	        })
93	        .unwrap_or_else(|| format!("ADOR {value}"))
94	}
95	
96	pub struct Config {
97	    pub port: String,
98	    pub baud: u32,
99	}
100	
101	/// What a `set-bin` step does to the binary output (register 75).
102	///
103	/// - `Fields` — `set-bin=<list>`: set the Common field mask to these fields
104	///   (and enable streaming if it was off).
105	/// - `Enable` — bare `set-bin`: (re)enable streaming with the device's current
106	///   mask.
107	/// - `Off` — `set-bin=off`: disable streaming, leaving the mask configured.
108	pub enum BinSet {
109	    Fields(Vec<&'static Field>),
110	    Enable,
111	    Off,
112	}
113	
114	pub enum Command {
115	    Help,
116	    Version,
117	    /// Read the ASCII async preset (register 6).
118	    GetAscii,
119	    /// Write the ASCII async preset (register 6); `off` disables it.
120	    SetAscii {
121	        preset: u8,
122	        persist: bool,
123	    },
124	    /// Write the ASCII async rate (register 7).
125	    SetAsciiHz {
126	        hz: u32,
127	        persist: bool,
128	    },
129	    /// Read the binary output config (register 75).
130	    GetBin,
131	    /// Configure the binary output field mask (register 75).
132	    SetBin {
133	        action: BinSet,
134	        persist: bool,
135	    },
136	    /// Write the binary output rate as register 75's rateDivisor (`800/hz`).
137	    SetBinHz {
138	        hz: u32,
139	        persist: bool,
140	    },
141	    SetBaud {
142	        baud: u32,
143	        persist: bool,
144	    },
145	    Reset,
146	    FactoryReset,
147	    /// Read any register (generic ASCII Read Register).
148	    Rrg {
149	        id: u8,
150	    },
151	    /// Write any register (generic ASCII Write Register).
152	    Wrg {
153	        id: u8,
154	        params: Vec<String>,
155	    },
156	    /// Configure an output (ASCII async by default, or binary with `--bin`) and
157	    /// measure the achieved rate, then restore prior state.
158	    ///
159	    /// - `serial_port` is the register-75 `asyncMode` (binary only): 1 or 2 for
160	    ///   one of the VN-100's two UARTs, 3 for both. Ignored for the ASCII bench.
161	    Bench {
162	        binary: bool,
163	        hz: u32,
164	        secs: u64,
165	        fields: Vec<&'static Field>,
166	        serial_port: u8,
167	        ascii_type: Option<u8>,
168	    },
169	}
170	
171	/// Format one help row: a `label` and one-or-more wrapped description lines,
172	/// aligned to a common description column. If the label is too wide, the
173	/// description starts on the next line.
174	fn help_row(label: &str, desc: &[&str]) -> String {
175	    const COL: usize = 24; // label column width; description starts after it
176	    let mut out = String::new();
177	    if label.len() <= COL {
178	        out.push_str(&format!("  {label:<COL$}{}\n", desc[0]));
179	    } else {
180	        out.push_str(&format!("  {label}\n"));
181	        out.push_str(&format!("  {:<COL$}{}\n", "", desc[0]));
182	    }
183	    for cont in &desc[1..] {
184	        out.push_str(&format!("  {:<COL$}{}\n", "", cont));
185	    }
186	    out
187	}
188

190	    let mut s = String::new();
191	    s.push_str("rw-vn100 - read/configure a VectorNav VN-100 over serial\n\n");
192	    s.push_str("Usage: rw-vn100 [--port PORT] [--baud BAUD] <command> [args]\n\n");
193

212	    s.push_str(&help_row(

message.content: I disagree, it may not be read now but surely will in the future, name it _groups or do a throw away read?

content.text:
You're right — that's parsed data we'll need the moment we support multi-group output; throwing it away is wrong. Let me reconsider the mechanism, though:

- **`_groups`**: on a *public field* this reads oddly, and the leading-underscore trick silences `unused_variables` (locals/params), not `dead_code` on a field — so it likely wouldn't even quiet clippy.
- **throwaway read** (`let _ = r.groups;`): a genuine no-op that future readers have to puzzle over.

The idiomatic "kept on purpose, not yet read" marker is an annotated `#[allow(dead_code)]` (or `#[expect(dead_code)]`, which clippy itself suggested — it auto-warns to remove the attribute once we *do* read it). That states the intent directly. Let me re-add the field that way. First let me see the current state of both `Reg75` constructions.
